rxn_class_summary: Nickel-catalyzed electrochemical conjugate addition of ortho-functionalized aryl bromides to Michael acceptors with sacrificial iron anode, followed by base-promoted cyclization to dihydro/tetrahydronaphthalenes.
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of Ni(II) (with 2 e-) generates a low-valent nickel species that undergoes oxidative addition into the Ar-Br bond; the sacrificial iron anode releases Fe(II) ions that act as a Lewis acid activating the Michael acceptor and the resulting carbanion/enolate intermediate.

key_intermediates.name: ArNi species from oxidative addition of o-(cyanomethyl)aryl bromide
Intramolecular ketenimine-type cyclized anion (from CN attack by benzylic carbanion)

electron_flow: At the cathode, Ni(II) is reduced by 2 e- to Ni(0), which oxidatively adds into the Ar-Br bond; the resulting aryl-nickel transfers the aryl group to the activated olefin, generating an α-EWG carbanion stabilized by Fe(II) (released by anodic dissolution of the iron anode), which either is protonated by EtOH to give the conjugate adduct or cyclizes onto a tethered CN/COCH3 group to give the bicyclic product.

probe_reagents.name: ethanol (proton source)
probe_reagents.observation: Addition of 1-4 equiv EtOH suppresses cyclization and favors the open-chain conjugate addition product (48-62% isolated); Fe(II) from anode enhances EtOH acidity.

probe_reagents.name: stainless steel anode (replacing Fe)
probe_reagents.observation: With non-sacrificial stainless-steel anode (runs 4-5), no Fe(II) Lewis acid is released and conjugate addition product is not observed (only cyclized 21, 22 formed), supporting the role of Fe(II) released from the sacrificial anode.

probe_reagents.name: excess acrylonitrile (4 equiv)
probe_reagents.observation: With o-acetyl aryl bromide 13 and acrylonitrile, double alkylation at the benzylic position is observed (compound 23), consistent with a benzylic carbanion intermediate (Scheme 7).

notes: Two competing pathways are proposed depending on conditions: (i) Ni-mediated aryl transfer followed by intramolecular cyclization of the α-EWG carbanion onto the ortho CN or COCH3 group gives bicyclic dihydro/tetrahydronaphthalenes (Scheme 6); (ii) when EtOH is added, the intermediate carbanion is protonated, giving the acyclic conjugate addition product that can be cyclized in a second step with NaOEt. Scheme 7 shows a competing benzylic dialkylation pathway with excess acrylonitrile. Fe(II) from the sacrificial iron anode is explicitly invoked as a Lewis acid activating both the Michael acceptor and EtOH.
